title: AI System Design Interviews
description: Master AI/ML system design interviews: structured approach, canonical design patterns, capacity estimation for LLM services, trade-off articulation, and worked examples for common senior AI engineering interview questions.
learningObjectives: Apply a structured 6-step framework to AI system design questions
Estimate capacity requirements for LLM APIs at scale
Design canonical systems: RAG search, LLM chatbot, AI content moderation
Articulate design trade-offs at the senior/staff engineer level
Identify and address common pitfalls interviewers probe for

sections.title: Why AI System Design Interviews Are Different from Traditional SDE Interviews
sections.content: Traditional system design interviews focus on distributed systems (caching, sharding, consistency). AI system design interviews add three layers: (1) ML model selection and trade-offs (latency vs quality, cost vs accuracy); (2) Data pipeline design (collection, labelling, versioning, training); (3) Production ML concerns (drift detection, A/B testing, monitoring, retraining). Interviewers expect you to reason about non-determinism, model behaviour, and feedback loops — not just infrastructure throughput. Senior AI engineers are also expected to quantify: 'at 1M requests/day with 2KB context each, how many GPU-hours do we need for inference?'

sections.title: 6-Step AI System Design Framework
sections.content:
**Step 1: Clarify Requirements (2-3 min)**
- What is the exact user problem? What constitutes a successful response?
- Scale: users/day, requests/day, peak QPS
- Latency requirements: real-time (<500ms), interactive (<2s), batch (minutes)
- Quality bar: accuracy/quality metric and target value
- Budget constraints: cost per request target
- Compliance: data residency, PII handling, regulatory requirements

**Step 2: Define Metrics (2 min)**
- Online metrics: P50/P95 latency, error rate, cost/request, user satisfaction
- Offline metrics: task-specific quality score, RAGAS for RAG, BLEU for translation
- Business metrics: session length, return rate, NPS

**Step 3: High-Level Architecture (5-7 min)**
- Draw the end-to-end system: input → processing → model → output → feedback
- Identify the critical path for latency
- Note data stores and their purpose

**Step 4: Deep Dive Key Components (10-15 min)**
- The interviewer will ask you to zoom in on one or two components
- Be ready to go deep on: retrieval, model serving, caching, safety, monitoring

**Step 5: Scale & Capacity (3-5 min)**
- Estimate: requests/day → requests/s at peak (assume 10× average)
- Token economics: context length × cost/token → $/request → $/day
- Infrastructure: GPU count for inference latency target
- Storage: vector index size, log retention

**Step 6: Trade-offs & Bottlenecks (5 min)**
- Cost vs quality: larger model = better quality, higher cost
- Latency vs throughput: batching increases throughput, increases latency
- Consistency vs availability: caching improves latency but may serve stale responses
- Safety vs utility: more aggressive filtering reduces harm but increases over-refusal

**Capacity Estimation Cheatsheet:**
```
LLM token cost:   1M tokens ≈ $0.15 (mini) to $2.50 (large)
LLM latency:      gpt-4o-mini: ~400ms P50; gpt-4o: ~1200ms P50
Embedding cost:   1M tokens ≈ $0.02 (text-embedding-3-small)
Vector search:    ~10ms for 1M vectors (HNSW, single node)
Kafka throughput: 100K events/s per broker
Postgres:         10K QPS for simple queries
Redis:            100K ops/s for cache lookups
```

sections.title: Capacity Estimator & System Design Templates
sections.code:
import math
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any


# ── Capacity estimator ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
@dataclass
class TrafficProfile:
    daily_requests: int
    peak_multiplier: float = 10.0  # peak QPS = daily / 86400 * peak_multiplier
    avg_input_tokens: int = 500
    avg_output_tokens: int = 200

    @property
    def avg_qps(self) -> float:
        return self.daily_requests / 86400

    @property
    def peak_qps(self) -> float:
        return self.avg_qps * self.peak_multiplier


@dataclass
class ModelSpec:
    name: str
    cost_per_1k_input: float   # USD
    cost_per_1k_output: float
    p50_latency_ms: int
    tokens_per_second: int     # throughput per model instance


MODELS = {
    'gpt-4o-mini': ModelSpec('gpt-4o-mini', 0.00015, 0.0006, 400, 5000),
    'gpt-4o':      ModelSpec('gpt-4o',      0.0025,  0.010,  1200, 2000),
    'claude-haiku':ModelSpec('claude-haiku',0.00025, 0.00125, 350, 6000),
}


class CapacityEstimator:
    def __init__(self, traffic: TrafficProfile, model_name: str):
        self._t = traffic
        self._m = MODELS[model_name]

    def cost_per_request(self) -> float:
        in_cost = self._t.avg_input_tokens * self._m.cost_per_1k_input / 1000
        out_cost = self._t.avg_output_tokens * self._m.cost_per_1k_output / 1000
        return in_cost + out_cost

    def daily_cost(self) -> float:
        return self.cost_per_request() * self._t.daily_requests

    def monthly_cost(self) -> float:
        return self.daily_cost() * 30

    def model_instances_needed(self) -> int:
        """Instances needed to handle peak QPS at target latency."""
        tokens_per_request = self._t.avg_input_tokens + self._t.avg_output_tokens
        tokens_per_second_per_instance = self._m.tokens_per_second
        requests_per_second_per_instance = tokens_per_second_per_instance / tokens_per_request
        instances = math.ceil(self._t.peak_qps / requests_per_second_per_instance)
        return max(2, instances)  # minimum 2 for HA

    def vector_store_size(self, n_documents: int, embedding_dim: int = 1536,
                           bytes_per_float: int = 4) -> str:
        """Estimate vector index size in GB."""
        size_bytes = n_documents * embedding_dim * bytes_per_float
        size_gb = size_bytes / 1e9
        return f'{size_gb:.2f} GB'

    def report(self, n_documents: int = 1_000_000) -> dict:
        return {
            'model': self._m.name,
            'daily_requests': f'{self._t.daily_requests:,}',
            'avg_qps': round(self._t.avg_qps, 1),
            'peak_qps': round(self._t.peak_qps, 1),
            'cost_per_request_usd': round(self.cost_per_request(), 6),
            'daily_cost_usd': round(self.daily_cost(), 2),
            'monthly_cost_usd': round(self.monthly_cost(), 2),
            'model_instances_for_peak': self.model_instances_needed(),
            'vector_store_size': self.vector_store_size(n_documents),
        }


# ── System design template: RAG-based enterprise search ───────────────────────
RAG_SEARCH_DESIGN = """
=== DESIGN: Enterprise RAG Search System ===

Requirements: 10K employees, 5M internal documents, P95 latency < 2s, 50K queries/day

ARCHITECTURE:
  [User] → [API Gateway + Auth] → [Query Router]
       ↓
  [Retrieval Pipeline]
    - Query expansion (multi-query, HyDE)
    - Hybrid search: BM25 + Dense embedding (HNSW)
    - Cross-encoder re-ranking (top-50 → top-5)
       ↓
  [Generation Pipeline]
    - Prompt construction (system + context + query)
    - Model: gpt-4o-mini for most, gpt-4o for complex queries
    - Safety guard (input + output)
       ↓
  [Response + Citation] → [User]

DATA PIPELINE:
  Documents → [Preprocessor: PDF/DOCX extract] → [Chunker: 150-token chunks]
           → [Embedder: text-embedding-3-large] → [Vector DB: Weaviate/Pinecone]
           → [BM25 Index: Elasticsearch]

CACHING:
  - Semantic cache: cosine sim > 0.95 → return cached response (30% hit rate)
  - Embedding cache: exact query hash → cached embeddings

MONITORING:
  - RAGAS metrics: faithfulness, context_recall per day
  - P50/P95 latency per pipeline stage
  - Cost per query
  - User satisfaction (thumbs up/down)

TRADE-OFFS:
  Speed vs Quality: cross-encoder re-ranking adds 300ms but lifts faithfulness 0.15
  Cost vs Recall:   larger embedding model costs 3× but improves recall 10%
  Freshness vs Cache: 24h TTL balances cost savings with document update lag
"""


# ── System design template: LLM customer support ──────────────────────────────
LLM_SUPPORT_DESIGN = """
=== DESIGN: LLM-powered Customer Support ===

Requirements: 100K users/day, 500K messages/day, CSAT > 4.0/5.0, auto-resolution rate > 60%

ARCHITECTURE:
  [User Message]
      → [Intent Classifier] (route to: FAQ, order, billing, escalation)
      → [Context Retrieval] (user history + relevant KB articles)
      → [Safety Guard] (input check)
      → [LLM Generation] (with streaming)
      → [Safety Guard] (output check + PII redaction)
      → [Response] + [Feedback Collection]

MODEL STRATEGY:
  - Simple FAQs → gpt-4o-mini (80% of traffic)
  - Complex/emotional → gpt-4o (15% of traffic)
  - Billing disputes → human escalation (5%)

MEMORY:
  - Short-term: conversation history in Redis (TTL: 24h)
  - Long-term: user preferences in PostgreSQL
  - Knowledge base: vector index of 50K support articles

FLYWHEEL:
  - Thumbs down → route to human + collect correction
  - 10K corrections/week → fine-tuning pipeline
  - Deploy fine-tuned model weekly via canary rollout

SLOs:
  - P95 TTFT < 500ms (streaming), P95 total < 5s
  - Availability > 99.5%
  - Safety violation rate < 0.1%
  - CSAT > 4.0 (measured from 5-star feedback)
"""


# ── Trade-off decision matrix ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
TRADE_OFFS = [
    {
        'dimension': 'Model size (quality vs cost/latency)',
        'use_small_when': 'High volume, simple tasks, latency-critical',
        'use_large_when': 'Complex reasoning, high-stakes, low volume',
        'hybrid': 'Route by complexity score; use small for 80%+',
    },
    {
        'dimension': 'Retrieval (precision vs recall)',
        'high_precision_when': 'Short context window, noise hurts quality',
        'high_recall_when': 'Missing documents is worse than extra noise',
        'hybrid': 'High recall in retrieval (top-50), re-rank for precision (top-5)',
    },
    {
        'dimension': 'Caching (freshness vs cost)',
        'cache_more_when': 'Stable content, high repetition rate',
        'cache_less_when': 'Frequently updated content, personalised responses',
        'hybrid': 'TTL based on document update frequency; semantic cache for FAQ',
    },
    {
        'dimension': 'Safety (security vs utility)',
        'strict_when': 'Healthcare, legal, children\'s products, high-liability',
        'permissive_when': 'Internal tools, expert users, low-harm domain',
        'hybrid': 'Tiered safety by use case; measure over-refusal rate',
    },
]


# ── Demo ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Capacity estimation
    traffic = TrafficProfile(
        daily_requests=500_000,
        peak_multiplier=8.0,
        avg_input_tokens=800,
        avg_output_tokens=300,
    )

    print('=== Capacity Estimates ===')
    for model_name in ['gpt-4o-mini', 'gpt-4o']:
        est = CapacityEstimator(traffic, model_name)
        report = est.report(n_documents=5_000_000)
        print(f'\nModel: {model_name}')
        for k, v in report.items():
            print(f'  {k}: {v}')

    # Cost comparison
    mini_est = CapacityEstimator(traffic, 'gpt-4o-mini')
    large_est = CapacityEstimator(traffic, 'gpt-4o')
    savings = (large_est.monthly_cost() - mini_est.monthly_cost())
    print(f'\nMonthly savings (mini vs large): ${savings:,.0f}')

    print('\n=== Trade-off Matrix ===')
    for t in TRADE_OFFS:
        print(f"\n{t['dimension']}")
        print(f"  Hybrid approach: {t['hybrid']}")

sections.title: Common AI System Design Interview Mistakes
sections.content:
**1. Starting with implementation details before requirements**
Jumping to 'use RAG with Pinecone' before clarifying scale and latency requirements shows poor design instincts. Always clarify: scale, latency, quality, cost, compliance.

**2. No numbers**
Saying 'we need caching to reduce load' without estimating the hit rate, cost savings, or QPS reduction demonstrates superficial thinking. Quantify: '30% cache hit rate reduces LLM cost by 30%, from $50K to $35K/month.'

**3. One-size-fits-all model selection**
Choosing 'GPT-4' for everything ignores cost and latency trade-offs. Show model routing: 'simple queries → gpt-4o-mini ($0.0001/req), complex → gpt-4o ($0.003/req), expected 80/20 split.'

**4. Ignoring the feedback loop**
Production AI systems must improve over time. Interviewers expect to see: feedback collection → dataset curation → retraining trigger → canary deployment → monitoring. Without this, the system is static.

**5. No failure modes**
Every system has failure modes. For LLMs: model API down (fallback chain), quality regression (canary rollback), cost spike (circuit breaker), jailbreak (safety layer). Address at least 2-3 failure modes.

**6. Safety as afterthought**
Adding a 'we could add safety filters later' at the end signals immaturity. For any user-facing LLM, safety is part of the core architecture: input guard, output guard, monitoring.

sections.title: AI System Design Practice: AI Code Review Tool
sections.problem:
**Design an AI-powered code review tool for GitHub PRs.**

**Constraints:**
- 10,000 PRs/day, average PR size: 200 lines changed
- Latency: comments posted within 60 seconds of PR creation
- Quality: false positive rate < 5% on security issue detection
- Cost: < $0.10 per PR review

**Required components to address:**
1. Architecture diagram: PR webhook → processing pipeline → comment posting
2. Model selection: which model(s), routing strategy
3. Context construction: how to handle large PRs exceeding context window
4. Quality gate: how to measure and enforce false positive rate
5. Capacity: QPS, model instances needed at peak, daily cost estimate
6. Failure modes: API down, slow response, false positive spike
7. Improvement loop: how does the system learn from developer feedback on comments

**Time limit: 45 minutes (interview simulation)**

seniorScenario.question: Estimate the infrastructure cost to serve a RAG chatbot for 1M enterprise users making 5 queries/day.
seniorScenario.answer: Traffic: 5M queries/day = 58 avg QPS, ~580 peak QPS. At avg 500 input + 200 output tokens, cost = 700 tokens × $0.00045/1k = $0.000315/query → $1,575/day = $47K/month for LLM alone. Embedding: 500 tokens × $0.00002/1k = $0.00001/query = $50/day. Vector search: 1M docs × 1536 dim × 4 bytes = 6 GB RAM index — single node. Total: ~$50K/month LLM + $5K/month infra = ~$55K/month for 1M users.

questions.question: You must design an LLM system for 1M requests/day at P95 < 1 second. What is the approximate peak QPS you must plan for?
questions.explanation: 1M/day = 11.6 avg QPS. With 10× peak multiplier = 116 QPS. (The 1,160 option would be a 100× multiplier, which is too high.) Peak is typically 5-10× average.

questions.question: At 500 input tokens + 200 output tokens per request with gpt-4o-mini ($0.15/1M input, $0.60/1M output), the cost per request is approximately:
In an AI system design interview, the most important thing to do in the first 3 minutes is:

options.C: Clarify requirements: scale, latency, quality, cost, compliance

questions.explanation: Requirements clarification is the foundation of every system design answer. Designing before clarifying requirements leads to solving the wrong problem.

questions.question: When asked 'how does your system improve over time?', the correct answer includes:

options.B: Feedback collection → dataset curation → retraining trigger → canary deployment → monitoring
options.C: We periodically update the system prompt
options.D: We increase the number of model instances

questions.explanation: The data flywheel is what makes AI systems self-improving. Interviewers expect to see the full loop: collect feedback → curate data → retrain → safely deploy → monitor for regression.

questions.question: A 1M-document HNSW vector index with 1536-dimensional float32 embeddings requires approximately:
questions.explanation: 1M × 1536 dim × 4 bytes = 6.14 GB. This fits on a single node with 16 GB RAM, including HNSW graph overhead (~2×). A critical capacity estimation data point.
